content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660              322-5901             322-6441             322-6444

September 10, 1981

William A. O'Malley, Esq.
District Attorney
Contra Costa County
Post Office Box 670
Martinez, CA 94553

Dear Mr. O'Malley:

     This letter is sent in response to your request for
advice regarding the reporting of gifts under the Political
Reform Act.  The following advice is provided pursuant to
Government Code Section 83114(b).1/  My understanding of the
facts is as follows.

     You are the District Attorney of Contra Costa County.
You file an annual Statement of Economic Interests pursuant
to Section 87200, et seq. disclosing investments, interests
in real property and income, which includes all gifts of
$25.00 or more in value.  You recently attended a trade
show at the MGM Grand Hotel in Reno, Nevada, sponsored by
Foremost-McKesson-Robbins, a drug company, at the invitation
of your sister and brother-in-law.  Your brother-in-law is
a sales executive in Chicago for Foremost-McKesson-Robbins.
During the weekend, Foremost-McKesson-Robbins paid for dinner
and show tickets for you and your wife one evening, and the
president of a firm that does business with your brother-in-
law paid for dinner for you and your wife the following
evening.  In addition, your hotel bill for the weekend was
paid by a travel agent in New York who had made the travel
arrangements for the trade show.  You requested advice
as to whether these items were reportable as gifts on your
Statement of Economic Interests.  Gifts are defined as all
payments to the extent that consideration of equal or greater

William A. O'Malley, Esq.
September 10, 1981
Page Two


value is not received. Section 82028. A gift from a
family member is reportable if the family member is acting
as the agent or intermediary for a person who is not a
family member. Section 82028(b)(3). Accordingly, each of
the above discussed items is reportable as a gift on your
annual Statement if its value was $25.00 or more from the
individuals or firms who made the payments; this is true
even though your brother-in-law arranged some of all of the
payments. In valuing these items for reporting purposes,
be advised that only the amount attributable to you need
be reported; the corresponding gift to your spouse is
not reportable. See Opinion requested by Kenneth Cory,
2 FPPC Opinions 48 (No. 75-094-A April 22, 1976) (copy
enclosed).

If I can be of further assistance, please feel free
to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Diane Maura Fishburn
Counsel
Legal Division

DMF:ce

Enclosure

1/ All statutory references made are to the Government
Code unless otherwise stated.

Office of District Attorney                 Contra
Court House, Fourth Floor                 Costa
P.O. Box 670                              County
Martinez, California 94553
(415) 372-4500

William A. O'Malley
District Attorney

[illegible]

August 24, 1981


Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Attention: Robert D. Blasier
           Enforcement Division

Dear Bob:

My brother-in-law is a sales executive in Chicago for
Foremost-McKesson-Robbins, the wholesale drug firm. McKesson
recently held a trade show at the MGM Grand Hotel in Reno,
Nevada.

Thousands of independent drug store owners from around
the United States attended the trade show. My brother-in-law
and my sister attended also. My wife and I spent the weekend
in Reno with them at their invitation.

Are the following items reportable:

1. McKesson provided dinner and tickets to the show
to all of the druggists attending. My brother-in-law obtained
free tickets for my wife and me. Total value approximately $50.

2. The next evening, the president of a firm that does
business with my brother-in-law paid the dinner check at the end
of the evening. Total cost approximately $50.

3. My brother-in-law arranged for payment of my hotel bill
by the travel agent from New York who made the travel arrange-
ments for the trade show. Total value was $116.

While we were guests of my brother-in-law and sister, the
above items were not paid by him, but by the firms with which he
does business. I had never met these people before this weekend.

Thank you for your assistance.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
WILLIAM A. O'MALLEY
District Attorney

WAO:cas

sections.question: Are the following items reportable as gifts on the annual Statement of Economic Interests: (1) Dinner and show tickets provided by Foremost-McKesson-Robbins valued at approximately $50; (2) Dinner paid by the president of a firm doing business with the requestor's brother-in-law valued at approximately $50; and (3) Hotel bill paid by a travel agent from New York valued at $116?
sections.conclusion: Each of the above discussed items is reportable as a gift on your annual Statement if its value was $25.00 or more from the individuals or firms who made the payments; this is true even though your brother-in-law arranged some or all of the payments. In valuing these items for reporting purposes, be advised that only the amount attributable to you need be reported; the corresponding gift to your spouse is not reportable.
sections.question_synthetic: The District Attorney of Contra Costa County sought advice on whether meals, entertainment tickets, and hotel accommodations provided by business associates of his brother-in-law during a trade show weekend in Reno, Nevada were reportable gifts under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: All three items are reportable gifts if valued at $25 or more each, even though arranged by a family member, because they were ultimately paid by non-family business entities. Only the value attributable to the requestor (not his spouse) need be reported.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Both incoming request letter (dated August 24, 1981) and FPPC response letter (dated September 10, 1981) were provided. Extracted question from incoming request and conclusion/advice from FPPC response. Document includes clear statutory references and an enclosed opinion enclosure notation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are the following items reportable as gifts on the annual Statement of Economic Interests: (1) Dinner and show tickets provided by Foremost-McKesson-Robbins valued at approximately $50; (2) Dinner paid by the president of a firm doing business with the requestor's brother-in-law valued at approximately $50; and (3) Hotel bill paid by a travel agent from New York valued at $116?

CONCLUSION: Each of the above discussed items is reportable as a gift on your annual Statement if its value was $25.00 or more from the individuals or firms who made the payments; this is true even though your brother-in-law arranged some or all of the payments. In valuing these items for reporting purposes, be advised that only the amount attributable to you need be reported; the corresponding gift to your spouse is not reportable.

embedding.summary: FPPC responded to the District Attorney's inquiry confirming that meals, show tickets, and hotel accommodations provided by business associates during a trade show were reportable gifts under the Political Reform Act, with the caveat that spousal portions were not reportable.
